metadata.title: Carbimazole-Induced Liver Injury: Review of Mechanisms, Clinical Presentation, and Therapeutic Management
metadata.description:
The incidence of carbimazole-induced liver injury (DILI) in patients treated for hyperthyroidism is infrequent, ranging from 0.1% to 0.5%. Whilethe injury usuallyfollows a cholestatic pattern, the condition can also present with mixed or cytolytic features, driven by either immune-mediated responses or the metabolic involvement of cytochrome P450 enzymes.We report the case of a 29-year-old woman with Graves’ disease (free T4: 78 pmol/L) who developed severe hepatotoxicity two weeks after initiating 40 mg/dayof Carbimazole. The patient presented with clinical jaundice and laboratory findings of significant cholestasis. After excluding viral, autoimmune, and structural etiologies, a diagnosis of carbimazole-induced hepatotoxicity was established.

Management included immediate cessation of the antithyroid drug, initiation of corticosteroid therapy (1 mg/kg/day), and a session of plasmapheresis to bridge the patient safely toward a thyroidectomy. Liver function showed marked improvement within one week of discontinuation. These findings suggest that when conventional cessation of the drug is insufficient to stabilize rapidly deteriorating liver enzymes, advanced extracorporeal therapies may serve as a crucial bridge to definitive surgical intervention. Ultimately, this report reinforces the necessity of vigilantmonitoring of liverfunction tests during the initiation of carbimazole therapy. By advocating for proactive serial biochemical screening, we aim to standardize safer clinical practices and improve outcomes for patients requiring rapid definitive treatment in the setting of severe, unpredictable, and potentially life-threatening drug-induced liver injury caused by antithyroid medications.

metadata.title: Metatranscriptomic analysis of lung and colon-faecal samples from bank voles (Clethrionomys glareolus)
metadata.description:
This technical note provides an overview of a proof-of-concept metatranscriptomic study conducted within the EU-funded BEPREP project (EU grant agree-ment no. 101060568) to characterise the RNA virome of bank voles (Clethrionomys glareolus) from ecologically distinct sites in Sweden and Italy. It describes sample selection, RNA extraction, sequencing, bioinformatic processing, and viral contig annotation from lung and colon-faecal samples, with particular attention to the detection of Puumala orthohantavirus-related sequences.

The technical document is named 20260530_metatrans_bv_BEPREP_R0.pdf. Associated raw data from the Illumina NextSeq 2000 sequencing run are named RNA_2025_013_FLI.tar. Details about the sequence raw data are provided in the technical note.

metadata.title: Comparative Analysis of Chlorophyll Pigments and Total Protein in Healthy and Virus-Infected Crop Leaves
metadata.description:
ABSTRACT

Plant viral infections significantly alter physiological and biochemical processes, particularly affecting photosynthetic pigments and protein metabolism in crop plants. The present study aims to evaluate the impact of viral infection on chlorophyll pigments and total soluble protein content in healthy and infected crop leaves. Healthy and virus-infected leaves of Solanum lycopersicum (tomato), Capsicum annuum (chilli), Lagenaria siceraria (bottle gourd), and Luffa acutangula (ridge gourd) were collected for analysis. Chlorophyll pigments were extracted using 80% acetone and quantified spectrophotometrically. Chlorophyll-a and chlorophyll-b contents were calculated using absorbance values measured at 663 nm and 645 nm following Arnon’s formula. The pigment contents of infected leaves were compared with healthy leaves to determine the biochemical impact of viral infection. The study revealed a reduction in chlorophyll content in infected leaves compared to healthy leaves. In tomato plants, chlorophyll-a decreased from 19.27 to 12.93 and chlorophyll-b from 25.60 to 23.31. In chilli, chlorophyll-a reduced from 23.36 to 11.67 and chlorophyll-b from 32.01 to 19.43. Bottle gourd showed a reduction of chlorophyll-a from 15.00 to 12.58 and chlorophyll-b from 27.76 to 21.66, while ridge gourd showed a decrease from 15.66 to 14.06 and 29.50 to 26.19 respectively. Protein estimation also showed variation between healthy and infected plants. Tomato protein OD decreased from 0.33 to 0.056 and chilli from 0.671 to 0.146, whereas bottle gourd increased from 1.071 to 1.278 and ridge gourd from 1.158 to 1.503, indicating possible stress-induced protein responses. The study demonstrates that viral infection significantly alters chlorophyll pigments and protein metabolism in crop plants. Reduction in chlorophyll content indicates impairment of the photosynthetic system, while variation in protein levels suggests activation of plant stress and defense responses. These findings highlight the potential of biochemical markers such as chlorophyll pigments and total soluble proteins for early detection and monitoring of viral stress in crop plants.

metadata.title: Pharmacophore-Based Virtual Screening and Molecular Docking of ZINC Database Compounds Against HIV-1 Integrase
metadata.description:
ABSTRACT

HIV-1 integrase is a critical enzyme involved in the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and represents an important therapeutic target in antiretroviral drug discovery. In this study, a pharmacophore-based virtual screening approach was carried out using Pharmit, with Dolutegravir as the reference drug. The pharmacophore model included aromatic groups, hydrogen bond donors, hydrogen bond acceptors (4), hydrophobic groups (2), and a negative ionizable feature. A total of 13,127,550 compounds from the ZINC database were screened, and the resulting hits were filtered using Lipinski’s Rule of Five, yielding 12 drug-like candidate molecules. Molecular docking was subsequently performed against HIV-1 integrase (PDB ID:3S3M) using AutoDock Vina. Among the screened compounds, ZINC000299738148 and ZINC000299738315 emerged as the top lead candidates, with binding energies of −9.7 kcal/mol and −9.4 kcal/mol, respectively. Notably, ZINC000299738148 exhibited a stronger binding affinity than the reference drug Dolutegravir (−9.5 kcal/mol), while ZINC000299738315 showed comparable binding performance. Both lead compounds demonstrated stable interactions with key active site residues, including ARG350, GLN215, HIS213, and SER216, similar to Dolutegravir, indicating a conserved binding mode. These findings suggest that the identified compounds may serve as promising inhibitors of HIV-1 integrase and warrant further investigation.

metadata.title: Pongaglabrone as a Potential Lead Molecule Targeting the Matrix Protein of Nipah virus: A Molecular Docking Study
metadata.description:
ABSTRACT

Nipah virus is a zoonotic pathogen was firstly identified in Malaysia country in 1998 and spread all over world including West Bengal, India in 2001. The severity is causing fatal infections and mortality in humans, it is very important to discover novel inhibitor with natural sources. The current Insilco study gives antiviral flavonoid against nipah virus. The Structure of nipah virus has surface glycoproteins (G and F) for host attachment and fusion and matrix (M) protein located beneath the viral lipid envelope that plays a crucial role in virus assembly, budding, and structural organization. The study focuses on matrix protein inhibition using flavonoids. The selected flavonoid-derived phytochemical Lanceolatin, Pongaglabrone, Pongaglabol, Flavone, and Isopongaglabol were evaluated for their inhibitory potential against the target protein represented by 7SKT protein structure using molecular docking analysis. Among the tested compounds, Pongaglabrone demonstrated the highest binding affinity with a docking score of −8.07 kcal/mol, followed by Pongaglabol with −7.14 kcal/mol. Isopongaglabol and Lanceolatin exhibited moderate binding energies, whereas Flavone showed the lowest binding affinity. The inhibition occurs in the more repeated binding regions PHE283, ARG286 and ILE243.These findings indicate that flavonoid-based phytochemicals, particularly Pongaglabrone, may serve as promising lead compounds for the development of antiviral agents targeting Nipah virus proteins. Further in vitro and in vivo studies are required to validate their therapeutic potential.

creators.name: Keerthana Chittimalla, Ashok vanigandla
creators.affiliation: Department of Biochemistry, Kakatiya University, Warangal-506009 TG, India

journal.title: The American Journal of Science and Medical Research

metadata.title: PETSc.jl: Julia bindings for PETSc
metadata.description: PETSc.jl provides an interface to the Portable, Extensible Toolkit for Scientific Computation (PETSc) library, allowing the combination of Julia features (such as automatic differentiation) with PETSc's infrastructure, including linear, nonlinear, and optimization solvers, timesteppers, domain management (DM), and more, in a distributed-memory (MPI) environment.

metadata.title: The Dismantled Mindset in the Age of Artificial Intelligence: Identity Fragmentation, Algorithmic Power, and a Framework for Collective Restoration
metadata.description:
The Dismantled Mindset in the Age of Artificial Intelligence:

Identity Fragmentation in Algorithmic Networks: A Mathematical

Model, Simulation, and Empirical Research Agenda

Felipe Castro Quiles, MBA

Independent Researcher | felipe@castroquiles.com

Working Paper, SSRN 5040267 / 5824582 | 2025

Abstract

This paper extends the Dismantled Mindset Theory (Castro Quiles, 2024, 2025) into the domain

of artificial intelligence and digital identity, arguing that algorithmic systems do not merely reflect

existing power fragmentation; they actively accelerate it. Grounded in the theory's four

foundational forces (greed, fear, influence, and responsibility) and its mathematical formalization

of fragmentation dynamics in directed social networks, this paper situates the Dismantled Mindset

construct within established research traditions in surveillance capitalism, filter bubble theory,

algorithmic identity curation, and the psychology of oppression. We propose four causal

mechanisms through which AI systems produce identity fragmentation, three falsifiable

hypotheses linking algorithmic exposure to measurable psychological outcomes, and a research

agenda for empirical validation. We further argue that the theory's normative commitment to

collective empowerment, rooted in the author's lived experience across the Americas (from San

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 1Juan to post-earthquake Haiti to the World Bank), provides a constructive dimension that critical

AI scholarship has largely lacked. The path from thought leadership to scientific theory runs

through measurement, peer scrutiny, and replication. This paper charts that path.

Keywords: Dismantled Mindset Theory, identity fragmentation, algorithmic power, AI ethics,

surveillance capitalism, collective empowerment, digital identity, social network dynamics, power

redistribution

1. Introduction

What if the very way we think about power, identity, and society is fundamentally flawed? What

if our understanding of these concepts is not just misguided, but a trap, one built upon greed, fear,

and manipulation, ultimately leading us to fracture our identities and limit our potential? These

questions, posed at the opening of the Dismantled Mindset Theory (Castro Quiles, 2024), are not

rhetorical. They describe a structural condition of contemporary digital life that has become

measurable, propagating, and urgent.

The relationship between technological systems and human identity has been a central concern of

social theory since the industrial era. What distinguishes the present moment is the degree to which

identity formation is now mediated not merely by institutions or social norms, but by opaque

algorithmic systems that continuously model, predict, and shape individual behavior at scale.

Social media recommendation engines, large language models, personalized advertising

ecosystems, and automated content moderation systems collectively constitute an environment in

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 2which the self is perpetually reflected back to the individual in curated, fragmented, and

commercially optimized forms.

The Dismantled Mindset Theory (DMT), introduced by Castro Quiles (2024) and formalized

mathematically in Castro Quiles (2025), provides a conceptual and quantitative framework for

understanding these dynamics. At the heart of the theory are four foundational forces: greed,

defined as the insatiable desire to control resources and power at the expense of others; fear, the

anxiety of losing power or status that compels self-preserving behaviors harmful to collective well-

being; influence, the capacity to inspire and lead through empathy and ethics; and responsibility,

the ethical obligation to use power for the greater good. These forces interact to produce what the

theory calls a dismantled mindset: a fractured sense of self and society that perpetuates division,

disempowerment, and inequality.

The theory emerges not from academic abstraction but from lived experience. The author's

trajectory, from a family business in Puerto Rico, through entrepreneurship in post-earthquake

Haiti, to policy dialogues at the World Bank and global innovation programs at Singularity

University, gives the framework a grounded empirical intuition that purely theoretical accounts of

AI and power often lack. The observation that algorithms had quietly shaped what people believe

before they start speaking, illustrated in a kitchen debate in Santurce between the author and his

uncle, both convinced they were seeing the full picture, both having been guided to their views by

invisible systems designed to feed them what they were already inclined to accept, captures in

everyday terms what the theory formalizes analytically.

Crucially, the Dismantled Mindset is described not only as a phenomenon to be diagnosed but as

one to be dismantled. The theory's central normative claim, captured in Quiles' Law of Dismantled

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 3Mindsets, is that only by breaking down fear, greed, and control can we rebuild ourselves and

unlock true potential. This dual character, critical and constructive, distinguishes DMT from most

critical AI scholarship, which tends toward diagnosis without prescription.

This paper has three objectives. First, it situates DMT within existing interdisciplinary literature,

demonstrating both its alignment with established findings and its distinctive contributions.

Second, it sharpens the theory's causal claims into falsifiable hypotheses. Third, it proposes a

research agenda that would enable DMT to move from a framework toward an empirically

validated theory of AI-mediated identity fragmentation.

2. Literature Review: Situating the Dismantled Mindset

The Dismantled Mindset Theory does not emerge in an intellectual vacuum. Several well-

established research programs address adjacent phenomena. This section maps the terrain,

identifying where DMT builds on, diverges from, and advances these traditions.

2.1 Filter Bubbles and Epistemic Fragmentation

Pariser (2011) introduced the concept of the filter bubble to describe how algorithmic

personalization progressively narrows users' informational environments, reinforcing existing

beliefs and limiting exposure to disconfirming perspectives. Subsequent empirical work has

complicated the picture: Guess et al. (2023) found that algorithmic effects on political polarization

are more modest than popularly assumed, and that user choice plays a substantial role in self-

selection into homogeneous information environments.

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 4DMT's contribution to this debate is to shift the unit of analysis from epistemic content, what

people believe, to identity coherence, who people understand themselves to be. The fragmentation

the theory describes is not primarily about holding incorrect beliefs, but about the progressive

incoherence of the self-concept under conditions of algorithmic curation. An individual who

encounters conflicting identity signals across algorithmically managed contexts does not simply

hold inconsistent beliefs; they experience a structural erosion of the narrative self. This distinction

is not addressed by existing filter bubble research.

2.2 Surveillance Capitalism and the Commodification of Identity

Zuboff (2019) offers the most comprehensive account of how the commercial logic of digital

platforms transforms human experience into raw material for behavioral prediction and

modification. Her concept of behavioral futures markets, in which platforms sell predictions about

future behavior to advertisers, implies a structural incentive for platforms to destabilize user

identity, since identity instability increases engagement and therefore generates more predictive

data.

DMT's power fragmentation framework is directly consonant with Zuboff's analysis but extends

it in two directions: first, it introduces a mathematical formalism for modeling fragmentation

dynamics across social networks rather than merely within individuals; second, it proposes a

constructive framework for collective restoration that is largely absent from Zuboff's critical

project. Where Zuboff describes the problem in its fullest form, the Dismantled Mindset Theory

attempts to specify the mechanism of reversal.

2.3 Algorithmic Identity and Context Collapse

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 5A growing body of work in human-computer interaction and critical internet studies has

documented how algorithmic systems shape self-presentation and identity performance. Bucher

(2018) examines how platform algorithms create an algorithmic imaginary, a set of beliefs users

develop about how algorithms work, which in turn shapes their behavior and self-conception.

Research on context collapse (Marwick and boyd, 2011) has shown that the flattening of social

contexts on platforms produces identity incoherence as users navigate incompatible audience

expectations simultaneously.

DMT's fragmentation construct maps productively onto this literature. The dismantled self

described by the theory is, in computational social science terms, a self whose context management

has broken down, an identity that has lost coherent narrative under the pressure of multiple,

algorithmically mediated audience contexts. The theory's four forces operationalize the specific

drivers: greed and fear on the platform side generate the commercial incentives for context

collapse, while influence and responsibility describe the individual and collective capacities

needed to resist it.

2.4 Structural Power and Identity: Sociological Antecedents

DMT's attention to structural power as the driver of identity fragmentation connects it to

foundational sociological work. Bourdieu (1986) describes how structural inequalities are

reproduced through differential access to symbolic resources. Foucault (1977) examines how

disciplinary power shapes subjects through normalized surveillance. Fricker (2007) addresses how

systemic power undermines the credibility and self-understanding of marginalized knowers.

The theory explicitly engages this tradition through its reference list, which includes Fanon's

account of colonial fragmentation of identity (Black Skin, White Masks), bell hooks's analysis of

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 6where class, race, and power intersect, Stuart Hall's work on cultural identity and diaspora, Ruha

Benjamin's Race After Technology, and Safiya Umoja Noble's Algorithms of Oppression. This

genealogy is not decorative; it positions DMT as extending the postcolonial and critical race

tradition into the computational domain. The invisible chains of legacy thinking that the theory

invites readers to examine are not merely psychological habits, they are historical sediments of

colonial power dynamics now encoded in algorithmic systems.

2.5 Collective Action and Liberation Psychology

The theory's normative commitment to collective empowerment situates it within liberation

psychology (Martín-Baró, 1994; Freire, 1970). These traditions argue that consciousness-raising,

becoming aware of structural constraints on one's identity, is a prerequisite for collective action.

DMT operationalizes this for the digital context: the author's concept of partnershoring, building

strong, enduring relationships that combine strengths to accelerate progress, is offered as a

concrete organizational strategy for enacting collective restoration. The theory's call for an

interdisciplinary, collaborative effort addresses the psychological, social, and technological

aspects of the Dismantled Mindset together, a scope that maps directly onto liberation psychology's

insistence that individual transformation is inseparable from structural change.

3. Theoretical Framework

3.1 Core Construct: The Dismantled Mindset

For purposes of empirical development, we propose the following operational definition, derived

from Castro Quiles (2024, 2025):

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 7A Dismantled Mindset is a psychological state characterized by high identity

fragmentation (f), low identity coherence (c = 1 − f), and reduced capacity for

autonomous self-determination, produced and sustained by exposure to

algorithmically mediated power structures that systematically distribute influence

(A) in ways that concentrate power (p) among a small number of nodes while

fragmenting the remainder. The state is driven by four interacting forces, greed

(G), fear (F), influence (I), and responsibility (R), whose aggregate effect on social

context (S) is captured by the Dismantled Mindset Score: DMS = k · (G + F - I +

R), where k is a normalization factor adjusting for cultural and societal context.

This definition has three components: a psychological component (identity fragmentation and

reduced self-determination); a structural component (algorithmically mediated power

concentration); and a dynamic component (the production and sustaining of fragmentation over

time through propagation across social networks). The DMS formula encodes the theory's key

insight, that influence (I) appears as a subtractive term, meaning that increases in genuine positive

influence counteract the fragmenting effects of greed and fear, while responsibility (R) adds

constructive obligation, whose effect depends on whether it is enacted or merely claimed.

3.2 The Mathematical Model

Castro Quiles (2025) formalizes fragmentation dynamics using a directed weighted network G =

(V, E), where nodes V represent individuals or social groups and edges E carry influence weights

A_ij from node i to node j. Each node carries a power attribute p_i ≥ 0, a fragmentation score f_i

∈ [0,1], and a coherence score c_i = 1 − f_i. Two governing equations describe the system's

evolution:

Fragmentation dynamics: Power redistribution: df_i/dt = α · Σ(j) A_ji · p_j · f_j − β · I_i

p_i' = (1 − γ) · p_i + (γ/N) · Σ(k) p_k

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 8In the fragmentation equation, α > 0 is sensitivity to external influence, I_i ≥ 0 is an intervention

applied to node i, and β > 0 is intervention effectiveness. The fragmentation of node i grows as a

weighted function of the power and fragmentation of its influencing neighbors, damped by

intervention. In the power redistribution equation, γ ∈ [0,1] governs the rate at which power

equalizes toward the network mean; a second equation A'_ij = (1 − δ) · A_ij for high-power nodes

captures the weakening of concentrated influence under structural intervention.

Simulation results (N = 10, T = 50) demonstrate that structural interventions systematically reduce

mean fragmentation and increase collective coherence. Crucially, high-power nodes drive

fragmentation propagation: peripheral nodes absorb fragmentation from structurally dominant

neighbors. This is the network-level mechanism underlying the theory's broader claim that AI

systems, by concentrating influence in algorithmically favored nodes, accelerate the dismantled

mindset at scale.

3.3 Four Mechanisms of AI-Driven Fragmentation

Building on the formal model, we propose four causal mechanisms through which AI systems

produce and sustain identity fragmentation:

Mechanism 1: Preference Amplification. Recommendation algorithms optimize for engagement

by amplifying existing preferences, progressively narrowing the range of identity resources

available to users and reducing the possibility of growth through exposure to difference. This

corresponds to α increasing as algorithmic personalization deepens.

Mechanism 2: Audience Multiplexing. AI-mediated platforms simultaneously present users to

multiple incompatible audiences, professional, intimate, political, commercial, creating chronic

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 9context collapse that undermines coherent self-presentation. This is the computational instantiation

of the Santurce kitchen observation: invisible systems guide what is believed before the person

begins to speak.

Mechanism 3: Behavioral Prediction Feedback. As platforms develop increasingly accurate

behavioral models of users, they surface content that confirms those models, creating a feedback

loop that constrains identity to the historically observed self and discourages growth, change, and

reinvention. This mechanism formalizes the observation in the book that many are drawn to

polished narratives that place certain visionary figures as the sole architects of tomorrow, an

algorithmically produced version of the sunk cost fallacy operating on identity rather than capital.

Mechanism 4: Influence Network Concentration. Small asymmetries in influence weight A_ij

compound over time to produce large concentrations of power in few nodes. AI systems, by

optimizing for engagement, structurally favor content from high-power nodes, influencers,

institutions, viral content, accelerating this concentration and reducing the influence of peripheral

nodes on their own identity formation. This is the formal expression of what the theory calls the

invisible hand of power dynamics.

4. Falsifiable Hypotheses

Based on the theoretical framework, we propose three falsifiable hypotheses as the core empirical

program for DMT development in the AI/identity domain:

H1 (Exposure-Fragmentation Hypothesis): Individuals with higher levels of algorithmically

curated social media consumption will report higher levels of identity fragmentation, measured by

a validated DMT Identity Fragmentation Scale, after controlling for baseline fragmentation,

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 10demographic variables, and general social media use. This tests the core mechanism that

algorithmic exposure drives the dismantled mindset.

H2 (Power-Coherence Hypothesis): Within social networks, nodes with higher eigenvector

centrality, a proxy for structural power consistent with the model's p_i term, will report lower

identity fragmentation and higher perceived autonomy over self-presentation. This tests the

model's prediction that high-power nodes accumulate coherence at the expense of peripheral

nodes.

H3 (Intervention-Restoration Hypothesis): Exposure to AI literacy interventions, education about

algorithmic curation mechanisms, consistent with the model's intervention term I_i, will produce

measurable reductions in identity fragmentation scores and increases in perceived collective

efficacy, mediated by increases in algorithmic reflexivity: the capacity to perceive and critically

evaluate the role of AI systems in shaping one's self-concept. This tests the theory's constructive

claim that identifying and naming the dismantled mindset is a prerequisite for reversing it.

5. Research Agenda

5.1 Scale Development: The DMT Identity Fragmentation Scale

The most urgent methodological priority is the development of a validated DMT Identity

Fragmentation Scale. We propose an initial item pool drawing on three adjacent validated

instruments: the Aspects of Identity Questionnaire (AIQ-IV; Cheek et al., 2002); the Identity

Distress Survey (Meca et al., 2015); and adapted items from the Digital Self-Presentation

Coherence literature (Chou and Edge, 2012). The DMS formula's four components, greed (G), fear

(F), influence (I), and responsibility (R), should each generate subscale items, allowing the

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 11composite score and its components to be estimated independently. A 24–32 item pool should be

administered to a diverse sample (target n ≥ 500) and subjected to exploratory factor analysis,

followed by confirmatory factor analysis in an independent sample.

5.2 Longitudinal Panel Study

H1 and H3 require longitudinal designs. A 12-month panel study with quarterly surveys measuring

identity fragmentation, algorithmically curated media consumption, perceived autonomy, and

collective efficacy would enable H1 testing. A pre-registered intervention arm, in which

participants receive a structured AI literacy curriculum based on the book's frameworks for

breaking free from old paradigms and recognizing the architecture of opinion, would enable causal

testing of H3. The book's observation that we think we're debating ideas but are often reacting to

curated versions of reality provides the conceptual basis for the literacy intervention content.

5.3 Network Calibration

The simulation's parameters (α, β, γ) are currently set to illustrative defaults. Fitting the model to

existing large-scale social network datasets, Twitter/X retweet networks, Reddit community

graphs, or platform engagement data, using maximum likelihood estimation, with identity

fragmentation proxied by behavioral indicators such as cross-community engagement diversity,

would generate falsifiable out-of-sample predictions for network-level fragmentation dynamics.

This would move the model from a simulation of the theory's assumptions to a tested predictive

model.

5.4 Collaboration and Publication Strategy

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 12The theory's interdisciplinary scope argues for a parallel publication strategy targeting distinct

academic communities. The empirical scale development paper is best suited to Journal of

Personality and Social Psychology or Personality and Individual Differences. The network

formalization belongs in Journal of Mathematical Sociology or Social Networks. The AI ethics

and critical theory dimension fits Big Data and Society or AI and Society. The applied leadership

and organizational dimensions connect to Academy of Management Review or Leadership

Quarterly. In each case, the reviewing community will ask for precise differentiation from adjacent

constructs, identity diffusion (Marcia, 1966), self-concept clarity (Campbell et al., 1996), and

algorithmic aversion, and the theory is strong enough to answer those challenges with the framing

developed here.

6. Discussion

Several limitations of the current framework merit acknowledgment. First, the construct requires

more precise differentiation from adjacent concepts, particularly identity diffusion, self-concept

clarity, and dissociation. The theoretical claim, that DMT fragmentation is structurally produced

by algorithmic systems rather than arising primarily from developmental or clinical factors, is

essential and must be demonstrated through discriminant validity testing, not assumed.

Second, the network model assumes directed weighted graphs with static node sets, an idealization

that omits important dynamics of platform design: feed algorithm changes, account suspension,

platform migration, and the emergence of new content modalities. Future model iterations should

incorporate these structural perturbations. The model's current deterministic form should also be

extended with stochastic components, as the author notes in the mathematical paper's discussion

of future work.

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 13Third, the theory's normative framework requires philosophical elaboration. Collective

empowerment toward what ends? The partnershoring concept and the calls for interdisciplinary

collaboration are compelling starting points, but they require institutional and policy translation to

generate specific recommendations for platform regulation, algorithmic transparency legislation,

or educational curriculum design. The Americas-rooted perspective, grounded in the author's

direct experience with governance, grassroots initiatives, and the intersection of technology and

marginalized communities from Puerto Rico to Haiti, gives the normative framework a specificity

and legitimacy that more abstractly theorized accounts of digital justice often lack. This regional

and experiential grounding should be made more explicit in future versions, as it constitutes one

of the theory's distinctive empirical resources.

The theory's most important long-run contribution may be precisely this combination: a formal

model rigorous enough to generate testable predictions, grounded in a normative framework

specific enough to generate actionable policy implications, and rooted in lived experience across

the communities most affected by the dynamics it describes. That combination is rare in either

critical AI scholarship or formal social network theory, and it represents the theory's strongest

claim to lasting relevance.

7. Conclusion

The Dismantled Mindset Theory offers a promising framework for understanding how artificial

intelligence systems contribute to identity fragmentation and the erosion of collective agency. Its

four foundational forces, greed, fear, influence, and responsibility, provide a structured vocabulary

for analyzing the power dynamics that algorithmic systems encode and amplify. Its mathematical

formalization provides a quantitative scaffold for generating and testing predictions. Its normative

Castro Quiles | Dismantled Mindset Theory | p. 14commitment to collective empowerment provides a direction for intervention that goes beyond

critique.

Up to today, it may seem like we are losing ground, with much of the world captivated by greed

and fear. Yet this moment holds a profound opportunity, possibly the final chance to guide the

future through the irreplaceable human virtues of courage, empathy, and moral responsibility. The

Dismantled Mindset Theory names the mechanism of that loss and sketches the pathway toward

recovery. The hard work of empirical development, measurement, calibration, peer review,

replication, is what will determine whether it becomes a scientific theory or remains a framework.

This paper has attempted to make the former possible.

The change-makers the theory envisions do not wait for the world to shift. They become the shift:

through imagination, purpose, persistent effort, and courageous action. The same is true for the

theory itself.

data.title: The Dismantled Mindset in the Age of Artificial Intelligence: Identity Fragmentation, Algorithmic Power, and a Framework for Collective Restoration

files.key: The Dismantled Mindset in the Age of Artificial Intelligence-  Identity Fragmentation in Algorithmic Networks- A Mathematical Model, Simulation, and Empirical Research Agenda  by Felipe Castro Quiles, MBA  .pdf

metadata.title: Encoding Genomes in Quantum States: A Conceptual Framework
metadata.description: Quantum computing offers a new paradigm for representing biological information, emphasizing the richness of quantum states rather than the size of qubit registers. This paper introduces a conceptual framework for genome encoding that extends the pioneering work of Sergii Strelchuk and colleagues, who demonstrated genomic representation with large qubit registers. By leveraging compositional biases in plant viral genomes—particularly adenine–thymine (AT) and uracil–adenine (UA) predominance—state‑based encoding achieves efficiency, compression, fidelity, and scalability. As a case study, we examine the Narcissus degeneration virus (NDV), an RNA potyvirus, and demonstrate codon mapping, reverse translation, and nucleotide‑to‑qubit assignment implemented on IBMQ hardware. This approach shows how fewer physical qubits can generate exponentially larger state spaces, enabling modular genome partitions within hardware constraints. Rather than replacing qubit‑based methods, state encoding complements and extends them, offering a natural evolution that honors foundational achievements while opening pathways for collaborative exploration in quantum genomics.

data.title: Encoding Genomes in Quantum States: A Conceptual Framework
